questions.question: What does 'data-driven decision making' mean?
options: Replacing all human judgment with algorithms
Using data and analysis to inform decisions rather than relying solely on intuition
Only making decisions when perfect data is available
Automating all business processes
questions.explanation: DDDM uses data as evidence to inform (not replace) human judgment. It acknowledges that data illuminates but doesn't dictate — context, values, and strategy still matter.

questions.question: Correlation between two variables proves that one causes the other.
questions.explanation: Correlation ≠ causation. Ice cream sales correlate with drowning deaths (both increase in summer — confounding variable). Proving causation requires: controlled experiments (A/B tests), randomization, or quasi-experimental designs.

questions.question: An A/B test compares a control group to a ___ group to measure the impact of a change.
questions.explanation: A/B testing: Control (existing version) vs Treatment (proposed change). Users are randomly assigned to ensure groups are comparable. Statistical significance (usually p<0.05) determines if the difference is real.

questions.question: Your A/B test shows the new checkout flow has a 3% higher conversion rate (p=0.04). But average order value dropped 8%. What do you do?

scenario.context: E-commerce checkout redesign. 50K users per group, 2-week test.

options: Launch new checkout — conversion increase is the primary KPI
Keep old checkout — revenue per visitor is the true business metric and it declined
Run the test longer — need more data for confidence
Launch for mobile only (where conversion lift was highest) and keep desktop version
questions.explanation: Revenue per visitor is the composite metric that captures both conversion AND value. The new flow converts more but at lower value (perhaps by removing upsells). Net effect is negative revenue. Always optimize for the metric closest to business value.

questions.question: A retention cohort: 1000 users sign up in January. 600 active in February, 420 in March, 336 in April. What's the month-over-month retention rate?
questions.explanation: Feb: 600/1000 = 60%. Mar: 420/600 = 70%. Apr: 336/420 = 80%. Average MoM retention ≈ 70%. Note: MoM retention is IMPROVING (60→70→80%) — this is a healthy pattern called 'flattening the curve'.

questions.question: Should companies use personal data to make decisions that affect individuals (pricing, credit, hiring) without their knowledge?
questions.scenario: A company uses browsing history, location data, and social media to personalize prices (charge more to wealthier zip codes), filter job applicants, and determine credit limits. Users never consented to these specific uses.

options.label: If users agreed to ToS, the data can be used for any business purpose
options.framework: Contract — consent was given via ToS

options.label: Each specific use requires separate informed consent
Personalization is fine for recommendations but not for consequential decisions (pricing, credit, hiring)
All personal data use should be opt-in with full transparency
options.framework: Data sovereignty — individuals control their data

questions.explanation: Emerging consensus (GDPR, proposed US legislation): low-stakes personalization (recommendations) needs basic consent. High-stakes decisions (credit, hiring, pricing) require explicit consent, transparency, and the right to opt out. The consequence determines the required consent level.

questions.question: What does the acronym 'KPI' stand for?
options: Knowledge Performance Indicator
Key Process Integration
Key Performance Indicator
Key Product Insight
questions.explanation: KPI stands for Key Performance Indicator — a metric specifically linked to a strategic business objective. Not all metrics are KPIs; a KPI is chosen because it measures progress toward what matters most to the organization.

questions.question: A metric and a KPI are the same thing — all metrics are KPIs and all KPIs are metrics.
questions.explanation: False. All KPIs are metrics, but not all metrics are KPIs. A KPI is a metric specifically selected to measure progress toward a critical strategic objective. Daily page views is a metric; if it's the primary measure of a brand awareness goal, it becomes a KPI.

questions.question: Which chart type is most appropriate for visualizing a trend over time?
options: Pie chart
Bar chart
Line chart
Scatter plot
questions.explanation: A line chart is ideal for showing how a value changes over time — it emphasizes continuity and trend direction. Pie charts show composition; bar charts compare discrete categories; scatter plots reveal relationships between two variables.

questions.question: A well-designed dashboard should display every available metric to give stakeholders the most complete picture possible.
questions.explanation: False. Effective dashboards focus on a small set of high-signal metrics (typically 5–9). Too many metrics cause cognitive overload, blur focus, and make decision-making harder. Context, relevance, and clarity matter more than completeness.

questions.question: What does a p-value measure in hypothesis testing?
options: The size of the effect being measured
The probability that the null hypothesis is true
The probability of observing results at least as extreme as those obtained, assuming the null hypothesis is true
The margin of error in the experiment
questions.explanation: A p-value is the probability of observing results as extreme as (or more extreme than) the data, given that the null hypothesis is true. A small p-value (e.g., p < 0.05) suggests the results are unlikely under the null — but it does NOT say the null is false or prove the alternative.

questions.question: In most business A/B tests, statistical significance is declared when the p-value is less than ___.
questions.explanation: The most widely used significance threshold is 0.05 (5%). This means there is less than a 5% probability of observing results this extreme by chance alone — conventionally considered strong enough evidence to reject the null hypothesis.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of data cleaning?
options: Compressing data to reduce storage costs
Encrypting data to protect user privacy
Identifying and correcting errors, inconsistencies, and missing values in a dataset
Visualizing data in charts and dashboards
questions.explanation: Data cleaning (also called data wrangling or preprocessing) is the process of detecting and fixing inaccurate, incomplete, duplicated, or irrelevant data. It's a prerequisite for reliable analysis — 'garbage in, garbage out' applies directly here.

questions.question: Predictive analytics uses historical data and statistical models to forecast likely future outcomes.
questions.explanation: True. Predictive analytics applies statistical algorithms and machine learning to historical data to predict future events, behaviors, or values — such as demand forecasting, churn prediction, or revenue modeling.

questions.question: What does SMART stand for in the context of setting effective business metrics?
options: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
Strategic, Manageable, Actionable, Realistic, Trackable
Simple, Meaningful, Accurate, Reliable, Timely
Scalable, Measurable, Automated, Responsive, Targeted
questions.explanation: SMART goals and metrics are Specific (clear definition), Measurable (quantifiable), Achievable (realistic), Relevant (tied to business goals), and Time-bound (have a deadline). This framework ensures metrics drive meaningful action rather than vague aspiration.

questions.question: What is the primary business value of automating analytics processes?
options: It eliminates the need for human analysts entirely
It reduces the cost of data storage
It scales repetitive data tasks and delivers insights faster with consistent quality
It makes AI decisions without requiring human oversight
questions.explanation: Analytics automation scales repetitive tasks (report generation, data refresh, anomaly alerts) so teams spend less time on manual work and more on interpretation and action. It improves speed and consistency, but human judgment remains essential for strategic decisions.

questions.question: Arrange these steps of the data-driven decision making process in the correct order, from first to last:
questions.explanation: The DDDM process: (1) Define the question — clarify what decision needs to be made. (2) Collect & clean data — gather relevant data and ensure quality. (3) Analyze data — apply statistical or ML methods. (4) Communicate findings — present insights clearly. (5) Take action & monitor — implement and track outcomes.

questions.question: Match each KPI framework or metric concept to its correct description:

right: Looks good but doesn't drive business decisions
Single most important measure of long-term growth
Four perspectives: Financial, Customer, Internal, Learning

questions.explanation: OKRs = Objectives and Key Results. Balanced Scorecard = Kaplan & Norton's 4-perspective framework. North Star Metric = the single metric that best captures core product value. Vanity Metric = looks impressive but doesn't inform meaningful decisions (e.g., total signups without activation data).

questions.question: A product team ran an A/B test for 2 days with 500 users per variant. Results show a 15% lift in conversion (p=0.03). What is the most significant concern with this experiment?
options: The p-value of 0.03 is too high to claim statistical significance
The test ran for too long and should have been stopped earlier
The sample size is likely too small and the duration too short — results are probably a false positive
Only mobile users should be included in the test
questions.explanation: 500 users over 2 days is almost certainly underpowered. Short tests miss weekly seasonality. A 15% lift and p=0.03 with this sample is likely a false positive — 'peeking' at results early dramatically inflates the false positive rate. Best practice: calculate required sample size before starting and run the full duration.

questions.question: In hypothesis testing, a Type I error is defined as:
options: Failing to detect a real effect that exists (false negative)
Correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis
Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true (false positive)
Accepting the null hypothesis when the alternative hypothesis is true
questions.explanation: A Type I error (α error) = false positive: you conclude there IS an effect when there actually isn't. The significance level (α = 0.05) controls the acceptable probability of a Type I error. Type II error (β) = false negative: missing a real effect. Type I errors lead to shipping changes that don't actually work.

questions.question: In time series analysis, ___ refers to predictable, repeating fluctuations that occur over a fixed period such as a year, month, or week.
questions.explanation: Seasonality is a systematic, calendar-related pattern in data — e.g., retail sales spike every December, traffic drops every weekend. Decomposing time series into trend + seasonality + residual is fundamental to forecasting. Failing to account for seasonality leads to poor predictions.

questions.question: Match each data quality dimension to its correct definition:

right: Data is up-to-date and available when needed
Data values accurately reflect real-world facts
No required fields or records are missing
Data is uniform across systems and sources

questions.explanation: Completeness = no missing required data. Accuracy = data matches reality. Timeliness = data is current when decisions need to be made. Consistency = same data produces the same results across different systems (no contradictions between databases).

questions.question: A high p-value (e.g., p = 0.8) proves that the null hypothesis is true.
questions.explanation: False. A high p-value means the data is consistent with the null hypothesis, but it does not prove it is true — you simply lack sufficient evidence to reject it. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. Underpowered tests routinely produce high p-values even when real effects exist.

questions.question: Which method provides the strongest evidence for establishing a causal relationship between two variables?
options: Correlation analysis of historical data
Cohort analysis over time
Randomized controlled experiment (A/B test)
Regression analysis controlling for confounders
questions.explanation: Randomized controlled experiments (RCTs / A/B tests) are the gold standard for causation because random assignment eliminates confounding variables. Correlation, cohort analysis, and even regression can suggest causation but cannot confirm it without proper experimental design.

questions.question: Arrange these steps of a structured decision-making framework in the correct order, from first to last:
questions.explanation: Structured decisions: (1) Define the problem clearly — a well-defined problem is half solved. (2) Gather relevant data — collect evidence. (3) Identify alternatives — generate options. (4) Evaluate and decide — weigh trade-offs and commit. Jumping to solutions before fully defining the problem is the most common decision-making failure.

questions.question: A marketing team's executive dashboard has 47 metrics. Every leadership meeting is spent debating which number to focus on, with no decisions made. What is the best course of action?

scenario.context: Weekly leadership meeting, 60 minutes. Executive dashboard has 47 metrics.

options: Add more metrics to ensure no data is overlooked
Replace the dashboard with a manual weekly email summary
Redesign to 5–7 key metrics directly aligned to strategic business objectives
Color-code all 47 metrics by RAG (red/amber/green) status
questions.explanation: Effective dashboards show 5–9 metrics at most. Redesigning around the 3–5 key questions leadership needs to answer weekly — tied directly to business objectives — eliminates debate and enables faster decisions. Cognitive overload from too many metrics is a common and costly dashboard design failure.

questions.question: A SaaS company with 100K active users wants a scalable modern data stack supporting real-time event tracking, historical analysis, self-serve BI, and ML features. Which architecture is most appropriate?
questions.scenario: Constraints: small data team, cloud-first, needs to scale, business users need self-serve BI access.
options: Excel + manual data exports from the production database
Operational database (PostgreSQL) directly connected to a BI tool with live queries
Event streaming (Segment/Kafka) → Cloud data warehouse (BigQuery/Snowflake) → dbt transformations → BI layer + ML platform
A single analytics monolith with embedded reporting
questions.explanation: The modern data stack separates concerns: event collection (Segment), storage & compute (BigQuery/Snowflake), transformation (dbt), and serving (BI tools + ML). This decouples analytics from production load, scales independently, enables self-serve BI, and supports both real-time and historical workloads.

questions.question: A product team celebrates: DAU increased 40% this month, a record. But 7-day retention dropped from 45% to 28% simultaneously. How should leadership interpret this?

scenario.context: Mobile app. A viral referral campaign launched this month.

options: The product is performing exceptionally well across all key dimensions
The retention drop is a temporary lag that will self-correct next month
A viral acquisition spike brought many new users who aren't finding core value — growth is fragile and doesn't reflect product health
The analytics system has a measurement error
questions.explanation: When DAU spikes but retention crashes, the most likely explanation is low-quality acquisition: viral campaigns brought mismatched users who don't convert to engaged long-term users. The North Star for product health is retained, engaged users — not raw acquisition. Leadership should focus on activation and onboarding, not celebrate the DAU spike.

questions.question: You surveyed 200 customers. 80 gave scores of 9–10 (Promoters), 60 gave 7–8 (Passives), and 60 gave 0–6 (Detractors). What is the Net Promoter Score (NPS)?
questions.explanation: Promoters = 80/200 = 40%. Detractors = 60/200 = 30%. Passives are excluded from the calculation. NPS = 40 − 30 = 10. An NPS of 0–30 is considered good; 30–70 is great; 70+ is exceptional. Scores below 0 indicate more detractors than promoters.

questions.question: An email marketing team's open rate crashed from 42% to 4% this month. No content changes were made. What is the most likely root cause?

presentation.context: Monthly email campaign, 500K sends, no strategy or content changes this month.
findings: Email service provider migrated to new sending infrastructure 3 weeks ago
Subject lines and send timing unchanged

options: The audience has lost interest — email fatigue set in rapidly
Subject line quality declined without the team noticing
Email deliverability problem — the 38% bounce rate indicates emails are not reaching inboxes, likely an IP reputation or domain authentication issue following the infrastructure migration
Seasonal decline in email engagement during this time of year
questions.explanation: The simultaneous crash in open rate AND spike in bounce rate (2% → 38%) after an ESP migration points clearly to a deliverability issue: new sending IPs haven't built reputation, or SPF/DKIM/DMARC records weren't updated. Emails are being rejected before users can open them. Content and audience are irrelevant until deliverability is fixed.

questions.question: A retail chain wants to forecast weekly demand for 10,000 SKUs across 200 stores to optimize inventory. Which modeling approach is most appropriate?
questions.scenario: Requirements: automated retraining, handles seasonality and promotional effects, interpretable for category managers, scalable to 2M SKU-store combinations.
options: A single global linear regression model applied uniformly to all SKUs
Manual trend analysis by category managers using spreadsheets
Hierarchical time series models (e.g., Prophet, LightGBM) with automated retraining, reconciled across store/SKU/category hierarchies
Simple 4-week moving average applied independently to each SKU
questions.explanation: At 10,000 SKUs × 200 stores = 2M series, manual approaches are impossible. Hierarchical forecasting leverages cross-series information. Automated retraining handles concept drift. LightGBM or Prophet capture seasonality, promotions, and holidays at scale. Hierarchical reconciliation ensures store + SKU + category forecasts are coherent.

questions.question: A company monitors employees via badge swipes, email metadata, meeting attendance, and software activity — without disclosure. HR flags 'disengaged' employees for review based on this data. What is the most ethically sound response?
questions.scenario: The practice is currently legal in the jurisdiction. Employees were never informed about the monitoring program.

options.label: Acceptable — the company owns the equipment and the ToS covers broad data use
Acceptable if disclosed in employment contracts going forward
Requires explicit informed consent; should never be used for punitive decisions without transparent criteria and appeal rights
Should be prohibited in all circumstances regardless of legal status

questions.explanation: Even where legal, covert behavioral surveillance used for punitive employment decisions violates core data ethics principles: informed consent, purpose limitation, and fairness. Emerging regulation (EU AI Act, state-level US laws) increasingly requires disclosure and restricts high-stakes automated people decisions. Ethical data practice applies regardless of current legality.

questions.question: A team wants to detect a 1 percentage point improvement in conversion rate (from 10% to 11%). Monthly traffic is 5,000 users. At 95% confidence and 80% power, what is the key constraint?
options: A 1 percentage point lift is too small to be practically meaningful for any business
The test cannot be conducted ethically with this traffic level
Required sample size (~150,000+ users per variant) far exceeds monthly traffic — the test would take 30+ months to reach significance
Statistical significance cannot be calculated for conversion rates below 15%
questions.explanation: For small baseline rates (10%) and small absolute lifts (1 pp), sample size requirements are enormous — typically 75,000–150,000 per variant. With only 5,000 monthly visitors this test is impractical. Solutions: accept a larger minimum detectable effect, increase traffic via paid channels, or use Bayesian sequential testing with proper early-stopping rules.

questions.question: A dashboard shows 'Total Revenue: $5.2M' with no time period, no comparison baseline, and no target. A new manager concludes the business is performing well. Which fundamental BI principle is violated?
options: The dashboard needs better color coding to highlight performance
A different chart type would communicate revenue more clearly
Context is essential — a number without time period, baseline, or target is uninterpretable and can actively mislead
Revenue is too sensitive a metric to display on a general dashboard
questions.explanation: Every metric requires context: (1) Time period — YTD, monthly, weekly? (2) Baseline — how does it compare to last period or year? (3) Target — are we on track? Without these, $5.2M is just a number. A year-ago comparison might reveal it's down 30%. Effective BI always provides context that enables correct interpretation and decisive action.

questions.question: A SaaS product has a 5% monthly churn rate. What is the approximate annual retention rate?
questions.explanation: Annual retention = (1 − 0.05)^12 = 0.95^12 ≈ 0.5404 = approximately 54%. This illustrates the power of compounding churn: a seemingly manageable 5% monthly churn loses nearly half the customer base every year. Reducing churn from 5% to 3% monthly would improve annual retention from 54% to 69%.

questions.question: A data analyst presents to the executive team with an 18-slide deck: model methodology, validation metrics, feature importance charts, and coefficient tables. Executives disengage after slide 4 and no decision is made. What approach would have been more effective?

scenario.context: Quarterly business review, 45-minute slot, C-suite audience of 6 executives.
scenario.outcome: Meeting ended with 'Let's follow up offline' — no decision made.

options: Add more statistical rigor and confidence intervals to build executive credibility
Present only the p-value and confidence interval on a single opening slide
Lead with the business recommendation and dollar impact, support with 3–5 clear insights, and reserve all technical methodology for an appendix
Eliminate all charts and use text-only bullet points for precision
questions.explanation: Executives need decisions, not methodology. The 'so what' (recommendation + business impact) must come first. Effective data storytelling: (1) Hook with the business question, (2) State the recommendation, (3) Support with 3–5 key insights, (4) Acknowledge risks. Methodology belongs in an appendix for those who need to validate it.
